title: Use the Break Apart Strategy to Add
essential_question: How can you use the break apart strategy to add 3-digit numbers?

pretest.question: Use the break apart strategy. 435 + 312 = ?

expected_errors.note: A: 400+300=700, 35+12=47, combined tens+ones without separating → 737

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.12 — 'Children who use invented decomposition strategies sometimes lose track of a part during reassembly; the hundreds digit of the first addend is the most frequently omitted component.'
expected_errors.note: D: used hundreds digit only (400+200=600), omitted a hundreds partial sum → 647
C: 400+300=700, 35+22=57 (wrong ones in second addend), wrote 757

pretest.hint: Add hundreds, then tens, then ones.

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.12 — 'Children who use invented decomposition strategies sometimes lose track of a part during reassembly; the hundreds digit of the first addend is the most frequently omitted component.'
expected_errors.note: D: 300+300=600 (wrong hundreds for second addend), 600+69=669

C: 300+135=435, not 425 — wrong tens in partial sum

pretest.question: Estimate, then use break apart. 283 + 541 = ? Which is the sum and a reasonable estimate?

expected_errors.note: B: chose 700+124=824 sum but estimate 200+500=700 instead of 300+500=800
C: sum correct (824) but estimate written as 900 instead of 800
D: estimate correct (800) but sum written as 724 instead of 824

pretest.hint: Estimate first by rounding, then break apart to find the exact sum.

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.27-28

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.title: What Is the Break Apart Strategy?
learn.content: The break apart strategy means splitting numbers by place value — hundreds, tens, and ones — and adding each place separately. Then you combine the partial sums to get the total.

visual_data.description: Base-ten blocks showing 355 (3 flats, 5 rods, 5 units) and 414 (4 flats, 1 rod, 4 units) being combined

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.25-28

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.math_note: Concrete intro — base-ten blocks model of break-apart

learn.title: Step 1: Estimate First
learn.content: Always estimate before you add. This helps you check if your answer makes sense. Example: 355 + 414. Estimate: 400 + 400 = 800. Your exact answer should be close to 800.

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.25-28

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.title: Step 2: Break Apart the Addends
learn.content: Break each number into hundreds, tens, and ones. Then add each place value. 355 + 414: Hundreds: 300 + 400 = 700. Tens: 50 + 10 = 60. Ones: 5 + 4 = 9.

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.25-28

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.title: Step 3: Add the Partial Sums
learn.content: Combine the sums from each place value. 700 + 60 + 9 = 769. Check: Is 769 close to the estimate of 800? Yes! So the answer is reasonable.

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.25-28

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.title: When Digits Add to More Than 9 (Regrouping)
learn.content: Sometimes when you add ones or tens, you get more than 9. Example: 257 + 168. Hundreds: 200+100=300. Tens: 50+60=110. Ones: 7+8=15. Now add: 300+110+15=425. The 110 means 1 hundred and 1 ten. The 15 means 1 ten and 5 ones.

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.25-28

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.title: Break Apart with Larger Sums
learn.content: Example: 476 + 385. Hundreds: 400+300=700. Tens: 70+80=150. Ones: 6+5=11. Total: 700+150+11=861. Estimate check: 500+400=900. Is 861 close to 900? Yes!

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.25-28

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.title: Why Estimate and Break Apart Together?
learn.content: Estimating first gives you a 'target.' If your break apart answer is far from the estimate, you probably made an error. Example: 283 + 541. Estimate: 300+500=800. Break apart: 200+500=700, 80+40=120, 3+1=4. Total: 700+120+4=824. 824 is close to 800 ✓.

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.25-28

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

learn.math_note: Estimate + break apart together: 283+541 → estimate 800, result 824 ≈800 ✓
learn.math_talk_prompt: You estimated 300+500=800 first, then found 824 using break apart. How did having that estimate help you? If your break apart answer came out as 624, what would you do next? Explain why combining 'estimate first' with 'break apart' makes your answer more reliable.

learn.title: Key Idea Summary
learn.content: The break apart strategy: 1) Estimate first. 2) Break each number into hundreds, tens, and ones. 3) Add each place value separately to get partial sums. 4) Combine the partial sums. 5) Check: Is your answer close to the estimate? This strategy works for any 3-digit addition!

practice_r1.question: Maya used break apart: 462 + 315 = 777. Her estimate was 500 + 300 = 800. Is her answer reasonable?

choices.D: Yes, because 777 is a lucky number

expected_errors.note: B: student confused reasonableness check — 23-off is fine but chose wrong option
C: estimate should be 500+300=800, not 400+300=700
D: confuses reasonableness with exact match requirement

solution_steps: 23 is a small difference, so yes, reasonable

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.15 — 'Stopping at an intermediate result is the most common error in two-step word problems at Grade 3; students often answer the implicit sub-question rather than the stated question.'
expected_errors.note: C: counted only Oak School students (389) — stopped at sub-result

practice_r2.hint: Estimate first. Then add each place.

practice_r2.question: Tom used break apart for 254 + 389. His work: 200+300=500, 50+80=130, 4+9=13. Then 500+130+13=633. Is Tom correct?

expected_errors.note: A: misidentified which partial sum is wrong — all three are correct
B: Sam's error is in final combination (630+13=643, Sam wrote 633)

expected_errors.citation: NCTM NBT Progressions p.11 — 'Even students who use correct decomposition strategies may revert to a digit-by-digit view when combining partial sums, omitting regrouping across the boundary.'
expected_errors.note: D: extreme distractor — if student thinks partial sums are wrong rather than reassembly

solution_steps: Tom wrote 633, but the correct answer is 643!
Tom made an addition error in the final step
practice_r2.hint: Check each step of Tom's work carefully.

practice_r3.question: A store sold 367 items on Friday, 458 on Saturday, and 175 on Sunday. What is the total? (Hint: add two first, then the third.)

expected_errors.note: A: 367+458=825, 825+175=1000 is correct; student wrote 990 (off by 10)
C: 825+185=1010 (wrong ones in third addend)

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.12 — 'Children who use invented decomposition strategies sometimes lose track of a part during reassembly; the hundreds digit of the first addend is the most frequently omitted component.'
expected_errors.note: D: added only two of three addends (367+458=825), omitted third addend

practice_r3.hint: Add 367+458 first, then add 175 to that result.

practice_r3.question: Sam used break apart for 254 + 389: '200+300=500, 50+80=130, 4+9=13. Total: 500+130+13=633.' What error did Sam make?

choices.B: He added the partial sums incorrectly — it should be 643
choices.D: The break apart strategy doesn't work for these numbers

expected_errors.citation: NCTM NBT Progressions p.11 — 'Even students who use correct decomposition strategies may revert to a digit-by-digit view when combining partial sums, omitting regrouping across the boundary.'
expected_errors.note: B: Sam's error — correctly found partial sums 500/130/13 but added 630+13=633 instead of 643
A: each partial sum IS correct; error is in final combination
C: estimating is helpful but not the source of the error here
D: break apart DOES work for 254+389; the strategy is valid

solution_steps: Sam wrote 633 instead of 643 — off by 10!
Sam made an error combining the partial sums
practice_r3.hint: The partial sums are right. Check the final addition.

practice_r3.question: Mia says: 'When I break apart 499 + 501, I get 400+500=900, 90+0=90, 9+1=10. So 900+90+10=1,000.' Is she correct?

choices.D: No, she broke apart 501 wrong — it should be 500+01

expected_errors.note: B: 900+90=990 — forgot the ones partial sum (9+1=10)
C: wrote 1,010 instead of 1,000 (added ones twice)

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.12 — 'Children who use invented decomposition strategies sometimes lose track of a part during reassembly; the hundreds digit of the first addend is the most frequently omitted component.'
expected_errors.note: D: broke apart ones incorrectly (501→0 tens 1 ones), dropped tens digit

practice_r3.hint: Add up Mia's partial sums carefully.

practice_r3.question: Which strategy is FASTEST for 398 + 400?

choices.D: Both A and B give 798, but B is faster

expected_errors.note: A: break apart gives correct answer but is NOT the fastest method
B: counting by tens gives correct answer but very slow for 398
C: friendly numbers gives correct answer and IS fast — partially correct

solution_steps: Both get 798, but friendly numbers is fewer steps
For numbers close to a hundred (like 398), friendly numbers is faster
practice_r3.hint: 398 is very close to 400 — which strategy takes advantage of that?
practice_r3.math_note: 398+400=798; friendly numbers (398→400, 400+400=800, 800-2=798) faster than break apart

concept_source.name: Go Math Grade 3 Chapter 1 Lesson 6 p.28 SMARTER / Enrichment

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Lesson 3-4 — break-apart (decomposition) addition strategy

assessment_source.name: NCTM Progressions NBT K-5 p.11 — partial-sum strategy and place-value decomposition

practice_r3.question: A school needs exactly 750 chairs. They have 387 in storage and can borrow 295 from another school. Will they have enough? Use break apart to decide.

expected_errors.note: A: 682 not 672; student got wrong partial sum (300+87+295 confusion)
C: 387+295=752 (wrong computation — hundreds error)

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.15 — 'Stopping at an intermediate result is the most common error in two-step word problems at Grade 3; students often answer the implicit sub-question rather than the stated question.'
expected_errors.note: D: found 682 chairs total but answered 'yes they have enough' — ignored the gap to 750

practice_r3.hint: Find the exact total, then compare to 750.

unit_intro_message: 이 단원에서는 세 자리 수 덧셈과 뺄셈을 1,000까지 확장합니다. 자리값을 분해하고 재조합하는 전략을 통해 수의 구조를 깊이 이해하세요.
unit_close_message: 단원을 마쳤습니다! 분해 전략(Break Apart)의 핵심은 각 자리의 부분합을 빠짐없이 구한 뒤 합산하는 것입니다.
